items.prompt_md: In `dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i])`, what does the `dp[i-2] + nums[i]` branch represent?
options: Rob house i, adding it to the best total through house i-2 (skipping house i-1)
Skip house i, carrying forward the best total through house i-1
Rob every house up to and including i
Skip both house i-1 and house i
items.answer: Rob house i, adding it to the best total through house i-2 (skipping house i-1)
items.explanation_md: Robbing house i forbids robbing house i-1, so the base you add it to must be the best total achievable through i-2.

items.prompt_md: What does the `dp[i-1]` branch of the recurrence represent?
options: Skip house i entirely, so the best total is whatever was already achievable through house i-1
Rob house i and house i-1 together
Rob only house i, ignoring everything before it
The average of all totals up to house i-1
items.answer: Skip house i entirely, so the best total is whatever was already achievable through house i-1
items.explanation_md: Skipping house i means nothing changes about the best strategy already found for the first i-1 houses.

items.prompt_md: Why is dp[i] the max of two branches rather than always taking the 'take it' branch?
options: Because taking house i is not always beneficial — if nums[i] is small relative to what robbing i-1 alone offers, skipping i is the better choice
Because the max() function is required by the language
Because house i must always be skipped if house i-1 was robbed
Because dp[i-1] is always larger than dp[i-2] + nums[i]
items.answer: Because taking house i is not always beneficial — if nums[i] is small relative to what robbing i-1 alone offers, skipping i is the better choice
items.explanation_md: The recurrence has to weigh both options explicitly because either one can be optimal depending on the values involved.

items.prompt_md: What base cases are needed to start the House Robber recurrence dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i])?
options: dp[-1] = 0 (nothing robbed before the array starts) and dp[0] = nums[0] (only one house available)
dp[0] = 0 and dp[1] = 0, since no houses can be robbed until index 2
Only dp[0] = nums[0] is needed; index -1 is never referenced
dp[0] = max(nums) and dp[1] = min(nums)
items.answer: dp[-1] = 0 (nothing robbed before the array starts) and dp[0] = nums[0] (only one house available)
items.explanation_md: With only one house, the best you can do is rob it; with zero houses before the array, the best total is 0 — these seed the recurrence for i >= 1.

items.prompt_md: Why does a plain running-sum approach (just add every nums[i] as you scan) fail to solve House Robber?
options: It ignores the non-adjacency constraint entirely, effectively robbing every house including adjacent pairs, which isn't allowed
It runs in exponential time
It requires the array to be sorted first
It only works when all values are positive
items.answer: It ignores the non-adjacency constraint entirely, effectively robbing every house including adjacent pairs, which isn't allowed
items.explanation_md: The whole difficulty of the problem is the adjacency restriction; a plain sum doesn't encode any 'take or skip' decision at all.

items.prompt_md: A student memoizes rob(i) = max(rob(i-1), rob(i-2) + nums[i]) top-down with an @lru_cache. What guarantees this runs in O(n) instead of exponential time?
options: The cache ensures rob(i-1) and rob(i-2) are each computed exactly once, no matter how many other calls also need them
The max() function inherently runs in constant time regardless of caching
Recursive calls in Python are automatically deduplicated by the interpreter
Because nums is a list, indexing is O(1) so the whole function must be O(n)
items.answer: The cache ensures rob(i-1) and rob(i-2) are each computed exactly once, no matter how many other calls also need them
items.explanation_md: Without the cache, rob(i-2) would be recomputed inside both the rob(i-1) branch and the direct branch, causing the same exponential blowup as naive Fibonacci.

items.prompt_md: Why can House Robber's O(n) array solution be collapsed to two rolling variables instead of a full dp array, just like climbing stairs?
options: Because dp[i] only ever reads dp[i-1] and dp[i-2], a fixed two-entry lookback, so older entries can be discarded once they're no longer needed
Because nums is guaranteed to be sorted
Because the answer only depends on the last house's value
Because max() cannot be applied to array slices
items.answer: Because dp[i] only ever reads dp[i-1] and dp[i-2], a fixed two-entry lookback, so older entries can be discarded once they're no longer needed
items.explanation_md: Same reasoning as Fibonacci-style DP: a small fixed lookback window is what makes the O(1)-space rolling-variable trick valid.

items.prompt_md: A buggy House Robber implementation computes dp[i] = dp[i-1] + max(0, nums[i] - dp[i-2]) instead of max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i]). What's wrong with this?
options: It changes the recurrence's meaning entirely — it no longer represents a clean 'best total through i' and can produce wrong answers even though it looks similar
It's mathematically equivalent, just written differently
It only fails when nums contains negative numbers
It's correct but slower due to the extra max() call
items.answer: It changes the recurrence's meaning entirely — it no longer represents a clean 'best total through i' and can produce wrong answers even though it looks similar
items.explanation_md: Small algebraic-looking rewrites of a DP recurrence are a common source of subtle correctness bugs; only the exact take/skip max() preserves the intended dp[i] definition.

items.prompt_md: For the circular variant (house robber II), where the first and last house are adjacent, why can't you just apply the linear recurrence directly to the whole array?
options: The linear recurrence assumes house 0 and house n-1 are never both robbed, but the circular constraint could otherwise allow that adjacent pair to be picked together
Circular arrays cannot be indexed with dp tables at all
The recurrence would need three previous terms instead of two
Circular arrays always have a different optimal answer of zero
items.answer: The linear recurrence assumes house 0 and house n-1 are never both robbed, but the circular constraint could otherwise allow that adjacent pair to be picked together
items.explanation_md: The standard fix is to run the linear solution twice — once excluding house 0, once excluding house n-1 — and take the max, so the wraparound adjacency is never violated.

items.prompt_md: Why is 'optimal substructure + overlapping subproblems' the right justification for DP here, rather than a greedy 'always rob the higher-value house'?
options: Whether to rob house i never depends on which specific earlier houses were chosen, only on the best totals through i-1 and i-2 — and those two subproblems recur across many decision paths, which is exactly what memoized/tabulated DP exploits
Greedy is actually correct here, but DP is used anyway to teach the pattern
Optimal substructure only applies when all values in nums are equal
DP is required whenever a problem statement uses the word 'maximum'
items.answer: Whether to rob house i never depends on which specific earlier houses were chosen, only on the best totals through i-1 and i-2 — and those two subproblems recur across many decision paths, which is exactly what memoized/tabulated DP exploits
items.explanation_md: A locally larger house value can block two smaller neighbors that together outscore it, so a greedy 'take the bigger house' rule is provably wrong in general.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the brute-force approach that tries every subset of houses avoiding adjacent picks?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
items.explanation_md: Checking every subset for the adjacency constraint means examining up to 2^n candidate subsets.

items.prompt_md: What is the time and space complexity of the tabulated House Robber solution using a full dp array?
options: O(n) time, O(n) space
O(n) time, O(1) space
O(n^2) time, O(n) space
O(log n) time, O(n) space
items.answer: O(n) time, O(n) space
items.explanation_md: One pass fills n dp entries before collapsing to rolling variables as a further optimization.

items.prompt_md: What is the time and space complexity of the rolling-variable (prev1, prev2) version of House Robber?
options: O(n) time, O(1) space
O(n) time, O(n) space
O(n^2) time, O(1) space
O(1) time, O(1) space
items.answer: O(n) time, O(1) space
items.explanation_md: One linear pass, two scalar variables carried forward instead of an array — O(n) time, O(1) space.

items.prompt_md: House Robber II runs the linear House Robber solution twice on two overlapping slices of length roughly n-1 to handle the circular constraint. What is the overall time complexity?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
items.answer: O(n)
items.explanation_md: Running an O(n) routine a constant number of times (twice) is still O(n) overall, just with a larger constant factor.

items.prompt_md: You must choose a subset of values from a line to maximize their sum, but no two chosen values may be adjacent in the sequence. Which approach fits?
options: At each position, take the max of 'skip it' (previous best) and 'take it' (best two back, plus this value)
Sort the values descending and greedily take the largest ones that don't conflict
Use a sliding window to track the maximum sum of any two adjacent elements
Binary search for the optimal subset size
items.answer: At each position, take the max of 'skip it' (previous best) and 'take it' (best two back, plus this value)
items.explanation_md: Greedy fails because a locally large value might block two smaller neighbors that together are more valuable; the take/skip recurrence weighs both branches and is guaranteed optimal.

items.prompt_md: Houses are now arranged in a circle, so the first and last are considered adjacent, and you still want the max non-adjacent sum. Which approach fits?
options: Run the linear take/skip dp twice — once excluding the first house, once excluding the last — and return the max of the two results
Backtrack through every circular arrangement and undo choices that violate adjacency
Binary search over the starting house
Two pointers, one fixed at the first house and one scanning forward
items.answer: Run the linear take/skip dp twice — once excluding the first house, once excluding the last — and return the max of the two results
items.explanation_md: Splitting into two linear sub-problems that each rule out one end of the wraparound pair reduces the circular case to two ordinary 1-D DP passes.

items.prompt_md: You must select items from a sequence to maximize total value, where selecting an item also forbids selecting either of its two immediate neighbors (not just one side). Which approach fits?
options: 1-D DP: define dp[i] with a lookback wide enough to capture the wider exclusion window, taking the best valid combination
Greedy: always take the single highest-value item first, then remove its neighbors from consideration
Two pointers converging from both ends of the sequence
Binary search over the total value achievable
items.answer: 1-D DP: define dp[i] with a lookback wide enough to capture the wider exclusion window, taking the best valid combination
items.explanation_md: Widening the adjacency constraint just widens the dp lookback window — it's still an overlapping-subproblems, take-or-skip decision at each position, so 1-D DP still applies; greedy can lock in a high value that costs you two better neighbors.

items.prompt_md: You need to determine whether a given binary tree is a valid binary search tree. Which approach fits?
options: Traverse the tree while tracking valid (min, max) bounds for each node, verifying every node's value falls within its inherited bounds
1-D DP: define dp[i] as whether the first i nodes visited in order form a valid BST
Two pointers scanning the tree's leaves from left to right
Greedy: always descend into the child with the larger subtree first
items.answer: Traverse the tree while tracking valid (min, max) bounds for each node, verifying every node's value falls within its inherited bounds
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: a BST-validity check is a tree-traversal problem with inherited bound constraints, not a linear take/skip decision — there's no 1-D sequence of overlapping subproblems here.

items.prompt_md: You must maximize total value picked from a sequence, where taking an item at index i also means you must skip exactly the next two indices (i+1 and i+2), not just the next one. Which approach fits?
options: 1-D DP: define dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-3] + value[i]), widening the lookback to match the wider skip rule
Greedy: always take the largest remaining value and skip forward blindly
Backtracking over every possible subset of picks
Binary search over the number of items picked
items.answer: 1-D DP: define dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-3] + value[i]), widening the lookback to match the wider skip rule
items.explanation_md: The take/skip structure is unchanged — only the lookback distance grows from 2 to 3 to reflect the wider forced skip, still solvable with the same DP shape.

items.prompt_md: You're given a singly linked list and must reverse it in place. Which approach fits?
options: Walk the list with prev/current pointers, redirecting each node's next pointer to point backward as you go
1-D DP: define dp[i] as the best total achievable from the first i nodes
Binary search over the list's midpoint
Backtracking through every possible ordering of the nodes
items.answer: Walk the list with prev/current pointers, redirecting each node's next pointer to point backward as you go
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: reversing pointers is a structural linked-list manipulation with no optimal-substructure decision or overlapping subproblem to memoize — plain pointer rewiring is both simpler and correct.

items.prompt_md: You must maximize the total value of items picked from a sequence, with the added twist that picking two adjacent items is fine as long as their combined value doesn't exceed a fixed cap — otherwise the take/skip constraint from House Robber applies. Which approach fits?
options: 1-D DP: extend dp[i] to consider both the 'skip' and 'take, respecting the cap' branches, still built from dp[i-1] and dp[i-2]
Greedy: take any pair of adjacent items whose sum is under the cap, ignoring the rest
Two pointers sweeping from both ends looking for pairs under the cap
Binary search for the cap value that maximizes total sum
items.answer: 1-D DP: extend dp[i] to consider both the 'skip' and 'take, respecting the cap' branches, still built from dp[i-1] and dp[i-2]
items.explanation_md: Adding a side condition to the take branch doesn't change the overlapping-subproblem shape — it's still resolved by comparing a small, fixed set of prior dp values.

items.prompt_md: You must find the length of the longest substring without any repeating characters. Which approach fits?
options: A sliding window that expands to include new characters and contracts from the left whenever a repeat is found
1-D DP: define dp[i] as the best non-adjacent selection ending at index i
Backtracking over every substring, checking each for repeats
Binary search over the substring length
items.answer: A sliding window that expands to include new characters and contracts from the left whenever a repeat is found
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: there's no take/skip decision with optimal substructure here, just a contiguous window that needs to stay valid — sliding window (a two-pointers technique) solves it directly in one pass.

items.prompt_md: You must select a subset of jobs, each with a start time, end time, and profit, to maximize total profit such that no two selected jobs overlap in time. Which approach fits?
options: 1-D DP: sort jobs by end time, and for each job take the max of 'skip it' and 'take it plus the best total from the last non-overlapping job before it'
Greedy: always take the job with the highest profit first, removing any jobs it conflicts with
Two pointers scanning jobs sorted by start time
Backtracking over every possible ordering of jobs
items.answer: 1-D DP: sort jobs by end time, and for each job take the max of 'skip it' and 'take it plus the best total from the last non-overlapping job before it'
items.explanation_md: Unlike plain interval scheduling (maximize count), weighting by profit breaks the greedy exchange argument — a lower-profit job that ends earlier isn't always better, so the take/skip dp (weighted job scheduling) is needed.

items.prompt_md: You must find the maximum sum of any single contiguous subarray within an array that may contain negative numbers. Which approach fits?
options: 1-D DP: define dp[i] as the best sum of a subarray ending at i, either extending the previous subarray or starting fresh at i
Two pointers narrowing a window from both ends of the array
Backtracking over every possible subarray
Binary search over the subarray's starting index
items.answer: 1-D DP: define dp[i] as the best sum of a subarray ending at i, either extending the previous subarray or starting fresh at i
items.explanation_md: The 'extend or restart' decision at each index depends only on dp[i-1], the same fixed-lookback overlapping-subproblem shape as house robber's take/skip choice.
